words.shortDefinition: Transmission Control Protocol; a reliable, connection-oriented protocol.
words.longDefinition: One of the main protocols of the Internet protocol suite. It provides reliable, ordered, and error-checked delivery of a stream of octets (bytes) between applications running on hosts communicating via an IP network.
words.techContext: TCP is used for applications where data integrity is paramount, such as web browsing (HTTP), email (SMTP), and file transfer (FTP).
words.exampleSentence: The application experienced high latency because of the TCP three-way handshake required for each new connection.

words.shortDefinition: User Datagram Protocol; a simple, connectionless communication protocol.
words.longDefinition: Unlike TCP, UDP does not guarantee delivery, order, or error-checking. It is used for time-sensitive applications where dropping a few packets is preferable to waiting for retransmissions.
words.techContext: We used UDP for our real-time video streaming service to minimize lag, even if it meant occasional frame drops.
words.exampleSentence: Online gaming and VOIP services primarily use UDP because low latency is more important than perfect data reliability.

words.shortDefinition: Domain Name System; the phonebook of the Internet.
words.longDefinition: Translates human-readable domain names (like stackspeak.com) into IP addresses (like 192.0.2.1) that computers use to identify each other on the network.
words.techContext: If you can't reach a website by name but can by its IP address, the problem is likely with your DNS configuration.
words.exampleSentence: I had to flush the DNS cache on my machine to see the changes after we updated our server's IP address.

words.shortDefinition: Internet Protocol; the set of rules for routing and addressing packets of data.
words.longDefinition: Responsible for delivering packets from the source host to the destination host based on the IP addresses in the packet headers. It defines the format of packets and the addressing scheme.
words.techContext: Every device connected to a network is assigned a unique IP address to facilitate communication.
words.exampleSentence: The firewall was configured to block all incoming traffic from that specific IP address range.

words.shortDefinition: Open Systems Interconnection model; a conceptual framework for networking.
words.longDefinition: Consists of seven layers: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, and Application. It helps standardize the functions of a telecommunication system without regard to its underlying internal structure and technology.
words.techContext: When troubleshooting a connection issue, we often start at Layer 1 (Physical) and work our way up to Layer 7 (Application).
words.exampleSentence: The router operates at Layer 3 of the OSI model, focusing on IP addresses and routing.

words.shortDefinition: Internet Control Message Protocol; used by network devices to send error messages and operational information.
words.longDefinition: A supporting protocol in the Internet protocol suite. It is used by network devices, like routers, to send error messages indicating, for example, that a requested service is not available or that a host or router could not be reached.
words.techContext: The 'ping' and 'traceroute' utilities both use ICMP messages to test network connectivity and path.
words.exampleSentence: I sent an ICMP Echo Request to the server to check if it was still online.
words.etymology: Acronym for Internet Control Message Protocol.

words.shortDefinition: Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol; automatically assigns IP addresses to devices on a network.
words.longDefinition: A network management protocol used on Internet Protocol (IP) networks for automatically assigning IP addresses and other communication parameters to devices connected to the network using a client–server architecture.
words.techContext: Your home router typically acts as a DHCP server, giving your laptop and phone their local IP addresses automatically.
words.exampleSentence: The laptop failed to connect to the WiFi because it couldn't reach the DHCP server to get an IP address.
words.etymology: Acronym for Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol.

words.shortDefinition: Transport Layer Security; provides authentication and encryption over a network.
words.longDefinition: The successor to SSL (Secure Sockets Layer). It encrypts the data sent between a web browser and a server, ensuring that the communication is private and hasn't been tampered with. It is the 'S' in HTTPS.
words.techContext: We upgraded our server to support TLS 1.3 to ensure the highest level of security and faster connection speeds.
words.exampleSentence: The browser showed a certificate warning because the server was using an expired TLS certificate.

words.shortDefinition: Secure Shell; a cryptographic network protocol for operating network services securely over an unsecured network.
words.longDefinition: Most notably used for remote login and command-line execution. It provides a secure channel over an insecure network in a client-server architecture, connecting an SSH client application with an SSH server.
words.techContext: I used SSH to log into the production server and manually restart the web service.
words.exampleSentence: To improve security, we disabled password-based SSH and now only allow logins using cryptographic keys.

words.shortDefinition: Simple Mail Transfer Protocol; the standard for electronic mail transmission.
words.longDefinition: A protocol used by mail servers and other message transfer agents to send and receive mail messages. While it is primarily used for sending mail, other protocols like IMAP or POP3 are used for retrieving it.
words.techContext: We integrated an SMTP relay service to ensure our application's transactional emails don't end up in users' spam folders.
words.exampleSentence: The email notification failed because our application couldn't connect to the SMTP server on port 587.
words.etymology: Acronym for Simple Mail Transfer Protocol.

words.shortDefinition: Address Resolution Protocol; maps an IP address to a physical MAC address.
words.longDefinition: Used to find the hardware address (MAC address) of a device from its known IP address. It operates between Layers 2 and 3 of the OSI model.
words.techContext: When a router wants to send a packet to a local device, it first broadcasts an ARP request to find which MAC address corresponds to that device's IP.
words.exampleSentence: I checked the ARP table on the switch to see which port the new server was connected to.

words.shortDefinition: Border Gateway Protocol; the protocol that makes the Internet work by routing data between networks.
words.longDefinition: An exterior gateway protocol designed to exchange routing and reachability information among autonomous systems (AS) on the Internet. It is responsible for finding the best path for data across the global network.
words.techContext: The massive Facebook outage in 2021 was caused by a BGP configuration error that essentially erased their servers from the Internet's map.
words.exampleSentence: Our ISP is experiencing BGP instability, which is causing intermittent routing issues for our users in South America.

words.shortDefinition: Network Address Translation; allows multiple devices to share a single public IP address.
words.longDefinition: A method of remapping an IP address space into another by modifying network address information in the IP header of packets while they are in transit across a traffic routing device.
words.techContext: Your router uses NAT to allow all the devices in your home to access the internet using the single IP address assigned by your ISP.
words.exampleSentence: We had to set up a NAT Gateway in our VPC to allow our private servers to download security updates from the internet.

words.shortDefinition: A number that defines the range of IP addresses that can be used in a network.
words.longDefinition: Used to divide an IP address into two parts: one that identifies the network and one that identifies the host. It helps routers determine if an IP address is in the local network or an external one.
words.techContext: A common subnet mask is 255.255.255.0, which means the first 24 bits are for the network and the last 8 bits are for local devices.
words.exampleSentence: I misconfigured the subnet mask, which prevented the server from communicating with other machines on the same local network.

words.shortDefinition: A network node that connects two different networks.
words.longDefinition: In a home or small office, the gateway is usually the router that connects the local network to the Internet. It acts as an entry and exit point for all data moving between the networks.
words.techContext: The 'Default Gateway' setting on your computer tells it where to send traffic that isn't destined for the local network.
words.exampleSentence: The internet connection was down because the gateway was unresponsive to requests.

words.shortDefinition: A security system that monitors and controls incoming and outgoing network traffic.
words.longDefinition: Establishes a barrier between a trusted network and an untrusted network (like the Internet). It uses a set of security rules to decide whether to allow or block specific traffic.
words.techContext: We had to open a hole in the firewall for port 8080 to allow our new API to be reachable from the outside world.
words.exampleSentence: The firewall blocked the attempted attack because it detected a series of suspicious connection requests from an unknown source.
words.etymology: Metaphor from physical firewalls designed to stop the spread of fire between buildings.

words.shortDefinition: An intermediary server that sits between a client and another server.
words.longDefinition: It receives requests from clients and forwards them to the destination server. It can be used for security, anonymity, caching, or to bypass regional restrictions. Types include forward proxies and reverse proxies.
words.techContext: We use Nginx as a reverse proxy to handle SSL termination and load balancing for our Node.js cluster.
words.exampleSentence: I configured my browser to use a corporate proxy server to access internal company resources from home.

words.shortDefinition: A device that distributes network traffic across multiple servers.
words.longDefinition: Ensures that no single server becomes overwhelmed by too many requests. It improves the availability and responsiveness of applications by spreading the work across a 'pool' of healthy servers.
words.techContext: Our load balancer automatically stops sending traffic to any server that fails its health check.
words.exampleSentence: The load balancer was configured with a 'round-robin' algorithm to distribute user requests evenly among the four backend nodes.

words.shortDefinition: The maximum rate of data transfer across a given path.
words.longDefinition: Commonly measured in bits per second (bps), bandwidth defines the 'width' of the pipe. High bandwidth allows more data to be sent simultaneously, but it does not necessarily mean low latency.
words.techContext: Our video streaming service requires at least 5 Mbps of bandwidth for a smooth high-definition experience.
words.exampleSentence: We upgraded our server's network interface to 10Gbps to ensure we have enough bandwidth for the data migration.

words.shortDefinition: The delay before a transfer of data begins following an instruction for its transfer.
words.longDefinition: In networking, it's the time it takes for a packet of data to travel from its source to its destination. It is often measured as Round Trip Time (RTT).
words.techContext: High latency can make a web application feel sluggish, even if the user has a high-bandwidth connection.
words.exampleSentence: Moving our servers to a data center closer to our users reduced the network latency from 200ms to 20ms.

words.shortDefinition: Round-Trip Time; the time it takes for a signal to go to a destination and back.
words.longDefinition: A key measure of network latency. It includes the propagation delay, processing delay, and queuing delay at each node along the path.
words.techContext: A high RTT is particularly damaging for TCP performance because it slows down the window scaling and congestion control algorithms.
words.exampleSentence: I ran a ping command to measure the RTT between my local machine and the database server.

words.shortDefinition: The most recent version of the Internet Protocol.
words.longDefinition: Designed to replace IPv4, it uses 128-bit addresses (allowing for 3.4×10^38 addresses) compared to IPv4's 32-bit addresses (4.3 billion). This solves the problem of IP address exhaustion.
words.techContext: IPv6 addresses are written in hexadecimal and separated by colons, such as 2001:0db8:85a3:0000:0000:8a2e:0370:7334.
words.exampleSentence: We enabled IPv6 on our load balancer to ensure we can support users who are on IPv6-only mobile networks.

words.shortDefinition: Maximum Transmission Unit; the largest size of a packet that can be sent over a network.
words.longDefinition: If a packet is larger than the MTU of a network segment, it must be fragmented (broken into smaller pieces), which can degrade performance and cause connectivity issues.
words.techContext: The standard MTU for Ethernet is 1500 bytes, but 'Jumbo Frames' can support up to 9000 bytes for better efficiency in data centers.
words.exampleSentence: We had to lower the MTU on our VPN interface because some routers along the path were dropping large encrypted packets.

words.shortDefinition: A method of grouping data into packets that are transmitted over a digital network.
words.longDefinition: Unlike circuit switching (like old phone lines), packet switching allows many devices to share the same network path by breaking data into small chunks that find their own way to the destination.
words.techContext: The entire Internet is based on packet switching, which allows for efficient and resilient communication.
words.exampleSentence: Packet switching allows our network to automatically reroute traffic if one of the intermediate routers fails.

words.shortDefinition: Virtual Local Area Network; a logical subnetwork that groups together devices from different physical LANs.
words.longDefinition: Allows network administrators to group hosts together even if they are not connected to the same network switch. This improves security and reduces broadcast traffic by isolating groups of devices.
words.techContext: We put our IP cameras on a separate VLAN to isolate them from the rest of the corporate network for security reasons.
words.exampleSentence: I configured the switch ports to be part of the same VLAN so the servers could communicate as if they were on the same physical cable.
words.etymology: Acronym for Virtual Local Area Network.
